title: Articles & Gender

primarySong.title: La Vida Es Un Carnaval
primarySong.note: Final song locked by team-lead at Gate 1 (2026-05-19). Title IS the lesson — 'La Vida Es Un Carnaval' bundles a feminine article+noun (la vida) and a masculine indefinite+noun (un carnaval) in five words. Pop = 'El Mismo Sol' (Alvaro Soler); reggaeton = 'El Perdón' (Nicky Jam & Enrique Iglesias); R&B = 'La Diabla' (Romeo Santos); regional Mexican = 'La Llorona' (Chavela Vargas).

notes: Module 7 audio set — narration-first per Reine's vision (locked 2026-05-15). Scripts lifted verbatim from module-007-content.ts.draft ttsTriggers array. M5/M6 gold-standard style — full English sentences with Spanish tokens embedded, single MP3 per cue, no slow/normal pairs. 9 narration cues + 6 reading-token cues = 15 files total. Reading-passage cap (6 unique tappables) enforced by team-lead at Gate 1. Speed rule applied strictly: all narration ≥ 4 words → speed 1.0 (no exceptions, M3/M6 stretching-artifact prevention). Reading-tokens: 'La Vida Es Un Carnaval' (5 words → 1.0), 'la canción' (2 words → 0.95), single-word articles (la/el/los/las → 0.9). Reading-token text matches readingPassage tappable text EXACTLY (case + accents) — verified character-by-character against draft lines 132-167.

ttsTriggers.text: Today we're learning the four little words that wrap every Spanish noun — el, la, los, las. They all mean 'the' in English, but Spanish splits them four ways: masculine singular, feminine singular, masculine plural, feminine plural. As you listen to 'La Vida Es Un Carnaval' by Celia Cruz, count how many times she sings la — it's the most common article in any love song, and once you can hear it, half of every Spanish chorus opens up. Press listen when you're ready.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, teacherly — sets up the lesson, primes the song
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Mixed-language opener. nova reads English as American English; Spanish tokens (el, la, los, las, La Vida Es Un Carnaval, Celia Cruz) with Spanish phonemes. 'La Vida Es Un Carnaval' (song title) must sound fully Spanish: la VEE-da es oon car-na-VAL — five clean Spanish words, NOT Anglicized. 'Celia Cruz' (SEH-lia kroos) is her stage name as commonly pronounced. The four article tokens (el / la / los / las) must each sound like a clean Spanish article — soft 'l', open Spanish 'a', closed 'e' in el. Estimated duration: ~28s.

ttsTriggers.text: El is 'the' for masculine singular nouns — el libro, the book. La is 'the' for feminine singular nouns — la casa, the house. The shortcut: if the noun ends in -o, it's almost always masculine and takes el. If it ends in -a, it's almost always feminine and takes la. Two endings, two articles, one rule that covers most of A1.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Three article+noun pairs to pronounce cleanly: 'el libro' (el LEE-bro — single-tap r), 'la casa' (la CAH-sa — soft s, open a). The article 'el' must be unaccented (closed 'e', short) — NOT the pronoun 'él'. 'la' has an open Spanish 'a' vowel. The endings discussion (-o / -a) should be enunciated as pure Spanish vowels, not English schwa. Estimated duration: ~17s.

ttsTriggers.text: Now the plurals. Los is 'the' for masculine plural — los libros, the books. Las is 'the' for feminine plural — las casas, the houses. One small wrinkle — if you have a mixed group of boys and girls, Spanish defaults to the masculine plural los. So los niños can mean 'the boys' or 'the kids' in general. Las niños is never correct.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: clear, instructive — teach-the-pairing tone with mixed-group rule
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Plurals must contrast clearly with singulars from prior cue. 'los' (lohs — short o, soft s), 'las' (lahs — open a, soft s). 'niños' (NEE-nyos — the ñ is critical, must sound like Spanish 'ny', NOT English 'n'). The closing line 'Las niños is never correct' is a deliberate teaching moment — 'las niños' should sound deliberately wrong (clean Spanish pronunciation, but the article/noun gender mismatch is the point). Estimated duration: ~21s.

ttsTriggers.text: Here's the deal. Spanish has no neutral nouns — every single one is masculine or feminine. The -o and -a endings give the gender away most of the time, but there are four big exceptions you should just memorize as pairs. El día — the day, masculine even though it ends in -a. La mano — the hand, feminine even though it ends in -o. El agua — the water, feminine but takes el to avoid the double-a sound. And el problema — the problem, masculine because of its Greek roots. Learn the rule first, then learn these four. That's it.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: clear, instructive — the conceptual crux of the lesson with four memorable exceptions
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Four article+noun exception pairs — each must sound clean and the pairing audible. 'el día' (el DEE-a — accent on the í, two syllables), 'la mano' (la MAH-no), 'el agua' (el AH-gwa — silent h, the gw glide is critical), 'el problema' (el pro-BLEH-ma — single-tap r, stress on the middle syllable). The 'el agua' note explains the phonetic rule (avoiding double-a 'la agua') — must be enunciated clearly enough that the learner hears why. Estimated duration: ~32s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'La canción es bonita.' — The song is pretty. Three words doing a lot of work. La is the feminine article because canción is feminine. Es is 'is', recycled from Module 6's ser. And bonita ends in -a because it has to agree with the feminine noun in front of it. If you say it about a masculine noun — like a book — it becomes 'El libro es bonito.' Same idea, different gender, different endings. Memorize the whole chunk — it's the most useful sentence you'll leave with today.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, conversational — modeling a real sentence the learner can use
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: The opening 'La canción es bonita' is the learner's takeaway — must sound like a natural declarative sentence, not a flat recitation. 'canción' (can-SYOHN — stress on the final syllable per the accent mark, the ó is critical), 'bonita' (bo-NEE-ta — open final a). The contrast pair 'El libro es bonito' (el LEE-bro es bo-NEE-to) must mirror the structure but flip the gender endings cleanly — bonita → bonito should be the only audible swap the learner needs to hear. Recycles 'es' from M6. Estimated duration: ~24s.

ttsTriggers.text: Try this. Look around the room you're in and pick five objects. For each one, say it out loud with its article. The book — el libro. The chair — la silla. The lamp — la lámpara. The phone — el teléfono. The music — la música. If you don't know the word, look it up — but always say the article and the noun together. Spanish nouns don't travel alone. They come wrapped in their article. From today on, that's how you say them.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, encouraging — coaching the learner to actually speak with everyday objects
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Five article+noun pairs must each be enunciated as a discrete unit with a brief comma-pause between them, so the learner can echo each one: 'el libro' (el LEE-bro), 'la silla' (la SEE-ya — the ll is a soft y in Latin American Spanish, NOT a Castilian ʎ), 'la lámpara' (la LAHM-pa-ra — stress on the first syllable per the accent), 'el teléfono' (el teh-LEH-fo-no — stress on the second syllable per the accent), 'la música' (la MOO-see-ka — stress on the first syllable per the accent). Three accent marks in five words — all dictate stress placement. Estimated duration: ~24s.

ttsTriggers.text: Celia Cruz was born in Havana, Cuba, and grew up to become the most celebrated voice in salsa music — a singer so iconic that the world simply calls her the Queen of Salsa. In 1998, on the album Mi Vida Es Cantar, she recorded a song whose title alone teaches today's whole lesson — La Vida Es Un Carnaval. Look at that title for a second. The first word is la — the feminine article — and it sits right in front of vida, a feminine noun. Three words later comes a masculine noun, carnaval, which would take el if it had a definite article in front of it. That's what Spanish does in every song, every sentence, every street sign. A noun never travels alone — it travels with an article, and the article tells your ear the gender before the noun even arrives. When Celia sings la canción, you hear a feminine word coming. When she names the carnaval, the masculine slot opens. Once you start hearing los and las in the chorus of any Spanish song, you'll realize Spanish has been teaching you gender from the first syllable.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone for the artist/cultural bio
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Long-form narration — 4+ word text MUST be 1.0 (donde-jugaran stretching prevention from M3/M6). Spanish proper nouns must be pronounced cleanly: 'Celia Cruz' (SEH-lia kroos), 'Havana' (heh-VAN-uh — English pronunciation since it's the standard English exonym in this sentence), 'Cuba' (KYOO-buh — English), 'Mi Vida Es Cantar' (album title, fully Spanish — mee VEE-da es can-TAR, four clean Spanish words), 'La Vida Es Un Carnaval' (song title, fully Spanish — la VEE-da es oon car-na-VAL, five Spanish words; appears twice in narration counting embedded reference to 'carnaval', must sound identical). The six Spanish tappable tokens (la, el, la canción, los, las, La Vida Es Un Carnaval) scattered through the passage must each be clean Spanish — these are the same tokens the learner will tap on screen. Estimated duration: ~70s at 1.0x.

ttsTriggers.text: If you picked reggaeton, your song today is 'El Perdón' — a global hit by Nicky Jam and Enrique Iglesias. The title is a clean masculine article+noun pair — el perdón, the forgiveness. Slower-tempo reggaeton, family-friendly lyrics, and both singers enunciate the el at the front of the title clearly. Listen for el and la in the chorus — every time you catch one, a gendered noun is arriving right behind it. Same lesson, different beat.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone, pair-toned with the reading passage
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: CONDITIONAL cue — only plays when user.genre === 'reggaeton'. Spanish-token QA: 'El Perdón' (el per-DOHN — single-tap r, stress on the final syllable per the accent), 'Nicky Jam' (English-stylized stage name, as commonly pronounced), 'Enrique Iglesias' (en-REE-keh ee-GLEH-syas — Spanish pronunciation). The repeated 'el' tokens in the script must sound consistent with the el used in the intro and vocab cues. Stretch goal — generate if budget permits, otherwise defer to a later batch. Estimated duration: ~28s.

ttsTriggers.text: From today, Spanish nouns never travel naked. You can wrap any noun you meet in its correct article — el libro, la canción, los chicos, las casas. You know the rule — -o is usually masculine, -a is usually feminine — and you know the four exceptions that break it. Next time you hear a Spanish song, listen for la and el in the first ten seconds. They're almost always there. And every time you catch one, you're hearing the gender of the next word arrive a beat early.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, celebratory — closing the loop, looking forward
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Closing cue — should feel like a coach giving a final pep talk, not a reading. The four-article roll-call (el libro / la canción / los chicos / las casas) should be paced with brief beats between each pair — comma rhythm, not rushed. The four nouns must each sound consistent with prior cues: 'libro' (LEE-bro), 'canción' (can-SYOHN — accented), 'chicos' (CHEE-kos), 'casas' (CAH-sas). Estimated duration: ~26s.
